name: Cáceres-Badajoz Corridor Nature Trail: Cáceres Loop
name_original: Desierto Corto — Casar de Cáceres y Arroyo de la Luz circular desde Cáceres
description: This moderate 30.1-mile road cycling route near Cáceres explores charming countryside, passing Laguna de Casar and rural towns.
region_intro: Road cycling around Cáceres is characterized by a diverse landscape, ranging from vast plains and fertile river valleys to challenging mountainous areas. The region features untouched mountain ranges, extensive oak forests, and unique *dehesa* ecosystems. Cyclists encounter varied topography, including dramatic gorges in Monfragüe National Park and granite rock formations near Los Barruecos Natural Monument. This mix of terrains provides a range of road cycling experiences across western Spain. Best road cycling routes around Cáceres The most popular road cycling route is Cáceres-Badajoz Corridor Nature Trail: Cáceres Loop, a 30.1 miles (48.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 8 minutes to complete. This route follows a designated nature trail, offering a moderate ride through the local landscape. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Pantano de Valdesalor — circular desde Cáceres, a moderate 31.1 miles (50.1 km) path. This circular route provides views of the Valdesalor Reservoir and surrounding terrain. Local road cyclists also love the Cáceres and Alcántara Reservoir Loop, an 81.5 miles (131.2 km) trail leading through the Alcántara Reservoir area, often completed in about 6 hours 30 minutes. Road cycling around Cáceres is defined by the *dehesa* ecosystem, the Sierras de Extremadura mountain ranges, and fertile river valleys. The network offers options for various ability levels, from routes across plains to challenging ascents. The routes in Cáceres are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 15 reviews. More than 1,500 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Cáceres's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Cáceres?
A: There are over 35 road cycling routes around Cáceres, offering a diverse range of experiences. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 15 reviews.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Cáceres?
A: Road cycling around Cáceres features a diverse landscape. You'll find everything from vast plains and fertile river valleys to challenging mountainous areas within the Sierras de Extremadura. The region also boasts unique &lt;em&gt;dehesa&lt;/em&gt; ecosystems and dramatic gorges, such as those in Monfragüe National Park, providing varied topography for cyclists.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Cáceres?
A: Yes, Cáceres offers several easy road cycling routes. Approximately 9 routes are classified as easy, perfect for beginners or those seeking a more relaxed ride. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1190809093/caceres-badajoz-corridor-nature-trail-caceres-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cáceres-Badajoz Corridor Nature Trail: Cáceres Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate trail that follows a designated nature path, offering a pleasant experience through the local landscape.

Q: Where can I find challenging road cycling routes in the Cáceres region?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Cáceres has approximately 9 difficult routes. These often involve significant ascents and descents through mountainous areas. A notable challenging route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1056074467/caceres-and-alcantara-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cáceres and Alcántara Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 130 km and includes substantial elevation changes, typically taking around 6 hours 30 minutes to complete.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Cáceres?
A: Yes, many routes around Cáceres are circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1190771720/pantano-de-valdesalor-circular-desde-caceres" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pantano de Valdesalor — circular desde Cáceres&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 50 km path that offers scenic views of the Valdesalor Reservoir.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while road cycling in Cáceres?
A: While road cycling in Cáceres, you can encounter a variety of natural and historical landmarks. Routes may pass by the unique granite rock formations of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6093807" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Barruecos Natural Monument&lt;/a&gt;, or offer views of the Valdesalor Reservoir. The region is also known for its &lt;em&gt;dehesa&lt;/em&gt; ecosystems and untouched mountain ranges, providing picturesque backdrops.

Q: Are there any historical sites or attractions near the cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. Cáceres itself is a UNESCO World Heritage site, and many routes start or end near its historic center. You can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4755551" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Centre of Cáceres&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/780383" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cáceres Main Square&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5674973" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torre de Bujaco&lt;/a&gt;. Further afield, routes might take you towards towns like Trujillo, known for its rich architectural heritage, or along sections of the ancient Vía de la Plata.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Cáceres?
A: The Extremadura region, including Cáceres, generally offers pleasant cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) are often ideal, with milder temperatures and beautiful landscapes. Spring is particularly stunning with cherry blossoms in areas like the Jerte Valley. Summers can be very hot, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended if cycling during this season.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling options in Cáceres?
A: While road cycling often involves shared roads, some routes are more suitable for families due to their moderate difficulty and scenic paths. The Vías Verdes (Greenways), which are repurposed former railway lines, offer traffic-free and relatively flat options ideal for family cycling, such as sections of the Vía Verde Ruta de la Plata.

Q: Where can I find amenities like cafes or places to rest along the routes?
A: Many road cycling routes in Cáceres pass through or near small towns and villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, and places to rest. Larger routes connecting Cáceres to other towns like Trujillo or those passing by reservoirs often have amenities available. It's always a good idea to plan your route and check for services in advance, especially on longer rides.

Q: Is parking available for road cyclists in Cáceres?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in and around Cáceres. For routes starting directly from the city, you can find public parking facilities. When starting from smaller towns or specific trailheads, there are often designated parking areas. It's advisable to check specific starting points for parking availability when planning your ride.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Cáceres?
A: The komoot community highly rates road cycling in Cáceres, with an average score of 4.8 stars. Cyclists often praise the region's diverse landscapes, from the unique &lt;em&gt;dehesa&lt;/em&gt; ecosystems and oak forests to the challenging mountain ranges. The blend of natural beauty with rich historical sites, such as the UNESCO World Heritage city of Cáceres, also makes for a memorable cycling experience.
tips: Well integrated into the city, it is located between Hernán Cortés Avenue and Rodríguez de Ledesma Avenue. It is a pleasant space with a large central pond and an Open-Air Sculpture Museum, which we can admire as we stroll through. At the main entrance, there is a botanical garden, and at the end, a fountain known as La Madrila, restored by the Escuela Taller (Workshop School) and once used as a drinking trough and wash house.
A beautiful corner of Casar de Cáceres, for a walk or bike ride. It has a bird observatory on its west face.

Malpartida de Cáceres is a small town along the national highway N-521 west of Cáceres. The town is the gateway to Los Barruecos Natural Monument, a series of lakes surrounded by a landscape of granite rocks.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Desierto Corto circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 48.5 kilometers (30.1 miles) long. Cyclists can generally expect to complete it in about 2 hours and 7 minutes, depending on their pace and stops.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as a moderate road ride. It involves an elevation gain of about 280 meters, making it suitable for cyclists with a good level of fitness.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain along the route is generally well-paved, making it ideal for road cycling. While the broader Cáceres region has varied landscapes, the areas around Casar de Cáceres are predominantly flat, contributing to a smooth ride.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll explore the charming countryside around Cáceres, featuring open plains and characteristic Mediterranean flora. The route passes through towns like Casar de Cáceres and Arroyo de la Luz, and you'll experience the natural and rural beauty of the Cáceres province, including the significant 'Dehesa de la Luz' near Arroyo de la Luz.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Desierto Corto cycling route?
faqs.answer: Key points of interest include Parque del Príncipe, Laguna de Casar, and the settlement of Malpartida de Cáceres. The route also offers a chance to experience the local culture in towns like Casar de Cáceres and Arroyo de la Luz, known for its 'Día de la Luz' festival.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling or hiking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route integrate with other paths. You'll find it intersects with parts of the Camino Natural Corredor Cáceres-Badajoz, the Mtb Vía de la Plata, and the Vía de la Plata hiking trail (02 Merida to Salamanca).

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Desierto Corto cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Cáceres. While specific parking areas aren't detailed, Cáceres offers various public parking options suitable for accessing the start of the circular route.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: To enjoy the best conditions, consider cycling during spring or autumn. The weather is typically milder, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter days of winter, making for a more pleasant ride through the countryside.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no special permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Desierto Corto — Casar de Cáceres y Arroyo de la Luz circular route. It's freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: Is the Desierto Corto cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily designed for road cycling. While dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, bringing a dog on a road bike can be challenging and is not typically recommended for this type of activity. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are safely secured and consider the fast-paced nature of road cycling.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for food or drinks along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through towns like Casar de Cáceres and Arroyo de la Luz, you'll find opportunities to stop for refreshments. These towns offer cafes and restaurants where you can refuel during your ride.
